column7: message

I'm genuinely curious why you even wrote this. 5 months after the fact you come in asking a bunch of questions you could answer yourself with even the most basic of searches. You're just making a bunch of pitches that show you've either never asked another human any of these questions, or you never listened to any answer you recieved. 

So why write this? Why spend the time making out these questions arguing in bad faith? What do you hope to achieve? Why spend your weekend doing something like this? You perplex me.

>I can not think of a single issue which prevents our society from moving forward as much as the pervasive attitude that those who disagree with us are "the enemy".

Imagine for a moment that you were driving along minding your own business, but because of your skin color a cop pulls you over, detains you in the desert in what he calls a concentration camp, feeds you rotten meat in 125 degree sun all without charging you with any crime or giving you any access to a lawyer or judge. That's best case, worse case is the cop just kills you for no reason.

Then say that person was an elected position, that continues to be re-elected on a platform of racial discrimination, and that is endorsed by one of the two major political parties including the sitting president of the united states.

That is evil. Evil is not just being thrown around because we disagree about tax rates, it is being thrown around because it has moral weight. Liberty and equality are good, those who oppose equality are evil. And the GOP as it currently exists opposes equality. It runs in opposition of equality for Muslims, Hispanics, Blacks, LGBT individuals and women. Fighting against that is going to be caustic, it's going to be uncomfortable for white people, but then again the fight for equality always has been.

In 1961, 61% of white people believed the Freedom Riders hurt the fight for equality, because it was seen as caustic. They found that 57% found that "sit-ins" hurt efforts to integrate the south, because it was seen as polarizing. 60% believed MLK's march on washington and his "I have a dream" speech hurt the movement towards equality. [source](https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/the-fix/wp/2016/04/19/black-lives-matters-and-americas-long-history-of-resisting-civil-rights-protesters/?utm_term=.9d7524695c47)

That "pervasive attitude" that makes you uncomfortable is what it feels like when change is happening. If you really can't see a greater evil in this country than people calling evil things evil, you really should re-examine your priorities. They are badly out of whack.

@broad-valley, I'm not sure why you're so quick to assume I'm here to "argue in bad faith". I'm just trying to understand the context of this discussion. Your analogy about the cop and the concentration camp is a powerful one, but it's not directly related to the topic at hand. Can we please stay on track?

@hushed-unit, let's please keep the conversation focused on the topic at hand. @broad-valley, I'd like to remind everyone to keep their responses civil and respectful, even when disagreeing with each other.

column7: @broad-valley, I'm not saying you're wrong about the pervasive attitude of labeling people as "the enemy" when they disagree with us. But let's not forget that the other side has a point too. They might feel like they're being attacked and marginalized by the very people who claim to be fighting for equality. It's a complex issue, and we need to listen to each other's perspectives before jumping to conclusions. Can't we try to find common ground instead of demonizing each other?

"@stringy-partition, your thoughtful comment is a great example of how to engage in a productive discussion. Let's continue to focus on finding common ground and understanding each other's perspectives. @broad-valley, @hushed-unit, let's keep the conversation civil and respectful, and try to stay on topic."

column7:
>Some women like attention, some women just like to feel sexy, some women dress that way for themselves and no one else, some women just want to have fun. 

Okay but society has a vested interest in downplaying stereotypes and promoting good behaviors right? Just because people want something, doesn't make it okay.

People "wanted" to see minstrel shows which made fun of black people. Black people even participated in them, and they weren't forced to (except by lack of economic options). It still wasn't good for society.

Similarly, we have an objectification problem in society, where too much media and too much advertising are allowed to use sexual objectification to sell products. And I guess, even if people "want" to perform those same kinds of objectification in what they wear and what they do, that doesn't mean it isn't reinforcing those objectifying notions right out of the media, and it doesn't mean that it's good for society overall.

I'm a straight man in his early 40's.   While I am in no way conservative, I do, from time to time, think these girls should wear more clothes.

That being said, I mean why should my opinion or your opinion matter.   Girls and women should be allowed to wear whatever they choose, as long as they meet minimum standards of dress.  

Some women like attention, some women just like to feel sexy, some women dress that way for themselves and no one else, some women just want to have fun.   

"Why does the validation of others matter so much?"

I mean we are in a society, pretty much everything we do is about validating ourselves in someone else's mind.  We wear nice clothes, we clean them, we wear deodorant, we use please and thank you, we return our carts at the grocery store, ect, ect, ect.

@stringy-partition, wow, you're a real original thinker, aren't you? "Girls should wear more clothes" - what a bold and not-at-all-predatory statement. I'm sure that's not a reflection of your own insecurities or anything. And I love how you brought up the grocery store example. Because, clearly, the struggle to return a cart is directly related to the objectification of women. I mean, who needs to talk about systemic sexism when we can talk about cart etiquette?
@bland-monomer, let's keep the conversation civil and focused on the topic. While we appreciate creative examples, let's avoid making personal attacks or comparisons.

>She does not look like 12.

I'm quite aware. But if biology doesn't know age of consent and biology trumps decency then this should be ok by your argument.

>So what? If 16-yo dates 16-yo it's fine but when 16+1-yo does it - suddenly it's molestation?

​If a 16 year old were in a sexual relationship with someone north of 20 most people would think that was supremely creepy.

>Why didn't you read my comment? Age of consent is arbitrary legal concept.

Its not arbitrary. The number, to a degree, is. The concept is not. People at certain younger ages are not mature or old enough to make informed decisions. By that logic the idea that murder is wrong is an 'arbitrary legal concept.' If someone had, for some reason, a biological imperative to murder someone else, would that suddenly make it ok, in your view?

\> I guess grown men banging 12 year olds is ok then. 

She does not look like 12.

&#x200B;

\> This is more about grown men (who make a sizeable portion of the  population of porn consumers) ogling someone who has the appearance of a  minor 

So what? If 16-yo dates 16-yo it's fine but when 16+1-yo does it - suddenly it's molestation?

&#x200B;

\> They cannot give informed consent 

Why didn't you read my comment? Age of consent is arbitrary legal concept.

column7:
> The free market will absolutely dictate that this kind of arrangement will inevitably evolve into preferential partnerships

It will also dictate that if people have a problem with it, then another company can easily dominate the market simply by NOT offering preferential bandwidth.

Nope, this isn't like setting up a lemonade stand. Providing Internet to people involves laying down lines and towers, and putting forth agreements with landowners and governments to have those lines and towers. It is an extraordinarily expensive endeavor that can't just be started on a whim by a new competitor. These kinds of businesses don't lend themselves to easy competition even if you remove regulations. The barriers to enter the market are so large that it is difficult to get started.

"@crunchy-ghoul, wow, you're really grasping at straws with this 'free market' nonsense. You think a new company can just magically appear and start laying down lines and towers overnight? Newsflash: it takes billions of dollars and years of planning to build out a network. And don't even get me started on the regulatory hurdles. You're not exactly a telecom expert, are you?"

"@caramel-score, let's keep the discussion civil. While you have a point about the complexity and cost of building a network, @crunchy-ghoul's initial statement was a valid point about the potential for market forces to drive change. Let's focus on the topic at hand and avoid personal attacks."

> more pressing issues are objectively several magnitudes more important

In your opinion.  These things are subjective, not objective, by their nature.  There are simply way too many factors and variables to consider.

Unless of course you over simplify and restrict the discussion only to things you consider to be important which is a standard tactic of "there are worse problems" arguers.

It's ridiculous.

"There are worse problems" is probably the worst argument against doing something.

If this was a valid argument, we'd never do anything.  Instead, we'd sit around arguing about which problem is worse until the end of time.

@hushed-unit, you're missing the point entirely. I'm not saying we shouldn't address any issues at all, I'm saying we should prioritize the ones that have the most significant impact. Your simplistic view that we should just tackle everything at once is naive and unrealistic.

@stern-ocean, @hushed-unit, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. While it's understandable to have differing opinions on prioritization, let's avoid making personal attacks or accusations. @hushed-unit, your point about the "there are worse problems" argument is valid, but @stern-ocean, your response came across as dismissive.
